    Write Great PRDs With This Skill Solution

    OK, let's kick this off. The initial prompt sent to the agent was:

    /grill-me I want to build an Instructor Analytics dashboard so that instructors have something to look at on their dashboard. Instructors need to see various types of metrics:

    - They need to see how their course is selling.

    - They need to see completion rates.

    - They need to see lesson drop-off points.

    Any other ideas that you can think of could be welcome.

    Rather than writing a PRD in a vacuum, we're going to have an interactive conversation that questions every assumption.

    Q1: Where does the dashboard live?

    Analytics can sit at different levels in the application. Each level answers different questions.

    The three options are:

    OptionLocationBest for
    APer-course tab under /instructor/:courseId"How is this single course doing?"
    BCross-course overview at /instructor/analytics"How is my whole catalog doing?"
    CBothComprehensive view with drill-down capability

    The recommendation was A (per-course first), but the choice here was B (cross-course overview). This makes sense for instructors managing multiple courses who want to see their overall business performance in one place.

    Q2: Replace or coexist with the current instructor page?

    The existing /instructor page shows a grid of course cards. Adding analytics could either replace it, sit alongside it, or blend into it.

    The options were:

    • A: Replace the courses grid entirely
    • B: Create a separate /instructor/analytics route
    • C: Hybrid - add a KPI strip above the existing courses grid

    The choice was B. This keeps the analytics dashboard separate and gives it room to grow without cannibalizing the course authoring surfaces.

    Q3: What metrics should we track?

    Not every metric is equally valuable. The schema supports many possibilities, from revenue and completion rates to quiz performance and geographic breakdowns.

    The transcript identifies tiered metrics:

    Tier 1 (core):

    • Sales and revenue
    • Completion rate
    • Lesson drop-off

    Tier 2 (high-value additions):

    • Average rating per course
    • Quiz performance
    • Geographic breakdown of sales

    Tier 3 (engagement signals):

    • Video drop-off within lessons
    • Comments and bookmarks volume
    • Coupon and team redemption stats

    After discussion, the scope locked to just revenue and rating. This keeps v1 focused and avoids the "big ball of mud" that analytics dashboards often become.

    Q4: How should we show revenue over time?

    Revenue can be displayed as a single number, a recent window with comparisons, or a time series that shows trends.

    The options ranged from:

    • A: Lifetime totals only (simple but tells no story)
    • B: Fixed recent window with delta comparison (30 days vs prior 30)
    • C: Time series with range selector (7d, 30d, 90d, all-time)
    • D: Both KPI and chart combined

    The choice was C, but without delta comparisons. The time series would use specific ranges: 7 days, 30 days, 1 year, and all-time. The selected period would be stored in the URL as a parameter, making the view bookmarkable.

    Q5: Aggregate chart or per-course breakdown?

    With multiple courses, the revenue chart could show all courses as a single aggregate line, multiple lines for each course, or mini-cards with sparklines.

    The decision: go with a single aggregate line to show overall business health. This keeps the chart clean and readable. Per-course performance details move into a sortable table below the chart.

    Q6: What columns should the table have?

    The per-course table is where detailed metrics live. Each column needs to be useful and not redundant with the main chart.

    The final column list:

    • Course title
    • List price
    • Revenue (for selected period)
    • Sales count (for selected period)
    • Enrollment count (for selected period)
    • Average rating and rating count

    Why separate sales and enrollments? Because team purchases create one sale but multiple enrollments. The table needs both numbers to tell the complete story.

    Status badges were removed. This is an analytics view of published courses, not a course authoring tool.

    Q7: Filter and consistency decisions

    Two clarifications ensure data consistency across the table.

    The decisions:

    • Show published courses only (no drafts, no archived). This keeps the table focused on revenue-generating courses.
    • Enrollments should be scoped to the selected time period, just like revenue and sales. Mixing lifetime and ranged numbers creates confusion.

    Q8: Should there be a KPI strip?

    Above the chart, a summary row of headline numbers helps readers understand the data at a glance.

    The KPI strip would show three cards:

    • Total revenue (for the selected period)
    • Total enrollments (for the selected period)
    • Average rating (lifetime, because rating is slow-moving)

    This creates a "headline" view before the reader engages with the more detailed chart and table.

    Q9: Chart granularity and type

    Charts need the right level of detail. Too many data points becomes noise; too few loses the story.

    The granularity plan:

    RangeBucketPoints
    7 daysDaily7
    30 daysDaily30
    12 monthsMonthly12
    All timeMonthlyvaries

    The chart type: a line chart, not bars. Lines show trend at a glance. Even zero-revenue periods render as explicit zero points, keeping the line continuous and honest.

    Q10: Which charting library?

    The repository uses shadcn/ui. The charting library could be the shadcn wrapper around recharts, bare recharts, or a custom SVG implementation.

    The choice was bare recharts. This avoids wrapping abstractions and keeps the dependency footprint minimal. One chart on one page doesn't warrant scaffolding primitive wrappers.

    Q11: How do users access the dashboard?

    The sidebar is where role-based navigation lives. Instructors need a clear path to analytics.

    The decision: a new sidebar item labeled "Analytics" below "My Courses", pointing to /instructor/analytics. This mirrors how other roles (students with Dashboard, admins with admin tools) access their key pages.

    Q12: URL defaults and empty states

    Two final details shape the user experience for new and inactive instructors.

    The decisions:

    • Default range: 30 days. This balances having enough data to show a trend without diluting it like 12 months or all-time.
    • Empty states: Show a single message ("No revenue data yet. Publish a course to start seeing analytics.") only when the instructor has no published courses OR zero lifetime purchases. If they have lifetime data but nothing in the selected range, render the page with zeros. This lets them switch to "All time" to see their historical data.

    The finished PRD

    After locking in all these decisions, the agent generates a complete Product Requirements Document.

    The PRD captures:

    • Problem statement: Instructors have no visibility into how their courses are performing
    • Solution: A dedicated cross-course revenue and ratings dashboard
    • User stories: 22 stories covering instructor and admin perspectives
    • Implementation decisions: Specific modules to build, tech choices, and data sources
    • Testing decisions: How to verify the feature works
    • Out of scope: Everything explicitly cut from v1 (completion rates, drop-off, geo, quizzes, deltas, etc.)

    This document now becomes the blueprint for breaking work into issues and beginning implementation.

    Why this process works

    The /to-prd skill uses Socratic questioning to pressure-test assumptions. Rather than accepting the initial requirements at face value, each decision gets interrogated. This reveals hidden complexity, prevents scope creep, and builds shared understanding between the planner and the team before any code gets written.

    The result is a PRD that's specific enough to build from, but not so prescriptive that it closes off good ideas during implementation.
